html:
[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 东方白 - Poetic description of dawn, evoking a serene and beautiful morning scene.
  • 女觉而起 - Common phrase in classical literature to describe a person's awakening.
  • 微语曰 - Means 'said softly,' indicating a gentle or whispering tone.
  • 君貌如冠玉 - Compliment meaning 'Your appearance is like a jade ornament,' suggesting beauty and nobility.
  • 何无丈夫气 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why do you lack the spirit of a man?' implying a challenge to the speaker's masculinity.
  • 得闻芗泽 - Means 'to hear the fragrance,' often used metaphorically to describe a pleasant or alluring scent.
  • 于愿至足 - Means 'to be fully satisfied,' indicating contentment.
  • 臣之好色 - Means 'my love for beauty,' but can also imply a broader appreciation of aesthetic qualities.
  • 不在床第间也 - Means 'not limited to the bedroom,' suggesting a more refined or spiritual appreciation of beauty.
  • 女微笑唾之 - Means 'the woman smiled and spat at him,' a vivid and somewhat unexpected action.
  • 湘钯承以襟 - Means 'Xiang caught it with his sleeve,' describing a specific action.
  • 须臾 - Means 'in a moment' or 'soon after.'
  • 成海棠一蒂 - Means 'became a cluster of hibiscus flowers,' a metaphorical transformation.
  • 异而问之 - Means 'wondered and asked,' indicating curiosity.
  • 女颦曰 - Means 'the woman frowned and said,' showing a change in her expression.
  • 君未识妾耶 - Means 'Do you not recognize me?' a rhetorical question.
  • 君畴昔所爱树 - Means 'the tree you loved in the past,' referring to a previous relationship.
  • 感君同死 - Means 'I was moved by your death,' expressing gratitude or affection.
  • 愿生生世世同作多情物 - Means 'I wish to be a loving being with you for countless lifetimes,' a declaration of eternal love.
  • 言次 - Means 'while speaking,' indicating a continuation of dialogue.
  • 夫人促召去 - Means 'the lady summoned him away urgently,' suggesting a sudden interruption.
  • 命侍者展绣幡招艭之 - Means 'ordered the attendant to spread out the embroidered flag to call the boat,' a formal and ceremonial action.
  • 飞花搅空 - Means 'flowers flew and stirred the air,' a poetic description of a magical or supernatural event.
  • 著湘衣袂间 - Means 'stuck to Xiang's sleeves,' indicating a physical interaction.
  • 不复脱落 - Means 'did not fall off,' suggesting a lasting effect.
  • 旋有暖风一缕起地上 - Means 'soon a warm breeze rose from the ground,' a poetic description of a natural phenomenon.
  • 顿觉身轻如叶 - Means 'suddenly felt as light as a leaf,' indicating a transformation.
  • 飘飘然惟风所向 - Means 'floating aimlessly wherever the wind blew,' suggesting a state of being at the mercy of external forces.
  • 顷之 - Means 'in a short while' or 'soon after.'
  • 触树而止 - Means 'touched the tree and stopped,' indicating a physical interaction.
  • 身乃与树合 - Means 'his body merged with the tree,' a metaphorical transformation.
  • 而枝叶动摇，无异臂指之使 - Means 'and the branches and leaves moved, just like the movements of arms and fingers,' suggesting a living, responsive entity.
  • 盖转生为海棠矣 - Means 'it turned out that he had transformed into a hibiscus flower,' a conclusion to the transformation.
  • 其旁有桃树，则女托焉 - Means 'there was a peach tree nearby, and the woman relied on it,' indicating a new relationship.
  • 于是相呼乐甚 - Means 'they then called to each other happily,' suggesting a joyful interaction.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 东方白 - Poetic description of dawn, often used in classical literature to evoke a serene and beautiful morning scene.
  • 微语 - A gentle or whispering tone, often used to describe intimate or delicate moments.
  • 冠玉 - A metaphor for beauty and nobility, often used to describe a handsome or noble person.
  • 丈夫气 - Refers to the spirit or qualities of a man, often associated with strength and courage.
  • 芗泽 - A metaphor for a pleasant or alluring scent, often used in poetry to describe beauty.
  • 床第间 - Refers to the bedroom, often used to describe intimate or sexual relationships.
  • 海棠 - A type of flower, often used in classical literature to symbolize beauty and love.
  • 绣幡 - An embroidered flag, often used in ceremonial contexts.
  • 艭 - A type of boat, often used in classical literature to describe transportation or travel.

Proper Nouns

  • 东方白 - Poetic description of dawn.
  • 女 - Refers to a woman or female character.
  • 湘 - The name of the male character.
  • 海棠 - A type of flower.
  • 绣幡 - An embroidered flag.
  • 艭 - A type of boat.
  • 桃树 - A peach tree.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 朱阑白砌，苔径横斜 - Describes a garden with red railings, white walls, and moss-covered paths, creating a picturesque scene.
  • 缭以短垣，垣有凿坏，通巨宅 - The garden is surrounded by a short wall with openings (likely gates or gaps) that connect to a large mansion.
  • 圃中花姊妹咸来问讯，款接甚欢 - The flowers in the garden (metaphorically personified as sisters) all come to greet, and the interaction is very friendly.
  • 月明风细，辄游戏清池碧草间 - Under the bright moon and gentle breeze, they play in the clear pond and green grass, suggesting a serene and joyful scene.
  • 情致殊凄宛也 - The atmosphere is unusually melancholic and tender, hinting at a deeper emotional undercurrent.
  • 未几桃始花 - Soon after, the peach trees begin to bloom.
  • 偕诸婢来观，各折枝簪鬓间 - She comes with her maidservants to admire the flowers, each of them picking branches to wear in their hair.
  • 谛视海棠，相谓曰 - They carefully observe the hawthorn (or crabapple) trees and speak to each other.
  • 何尚未蕊 - Why haven't the buds appeared yet?
  • 湘即欲具蕊 - Xiang (presumably another character) wants to make the buds appear.
  • 女止之曰 - The daughter stops her and says.
  • 君花期尚在半月后，何遽也 - Your flower's blooming period is still two weeks away. Why rush?
  • 违候而花，将不寿矣 - If you make it bloom out of season, it won't last long.
  • 湘不听，明日花焉 - Xiang doesn't listen, and the next day the flower blooms.
  • 雪燕不意其猝开，数日竟不至 - Xueyan was not expecting it to bloom so suddenly and for several days she does not come.
  • 落矣，又开以待之 - It falls, and then blooms again to wait for her.
  • 三开，雪燕来，惊曰 - After blooming three times, Xueyan comes, surprised and says.
  • 何遽若此 - Why did it bloom so quickly?
  • 徘徊久之，折数枝，作胆瓶供 - She lingers for a long time, picking several branches to arrange in a vase.
  • 湘不胜喜 - Xiang is extremely happy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贵绅 - Refers to a wealthy and influential gentleman, a common figure in classical Chinese literature.
  • 花圃 - A garden, often used in literature to symbolize beauty and tranquility.
  • 花姊妹 - Personification of flowers as sisters, a common literary device to anthropomorphize nature.
  • 月明风细 - A poetic description of a clear night with a gentle breeze, often used to set a serene and romantic mood.
  • 清池碧草 - Clear pond and green grass, evoking a peaceful and natural setting.
  • 情致殊凄宛 - An unusual combination of melancholy and tenderness, suggesting a complex emotional state.
  • 桃始花 - Peach blossoms, often associated with spring and renewal.
  • 雪燕 - The name of the daughter, which may have symbolic meaning (e.g., like a snowy swallow).
  • 海棠 - Hawthorn or crabapple, often used in poetry to symbolize beauty and purity.
  • 花期 - The blooming period of a flower, often used metaphorically to describe the timing of events.
  • 违候而花 - Making a flower bloom out of season, which can be seen as unnatural or risky.
  • 不寿 - Not lasting long, suggesting that artificial intervention can have negative consequences.
  • 胆瓶 - A type of vase, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe elegant floral arrangements.

Proper Nouns

  • 湘 - A character in the story, likely a close friend or companion of Xueyan.
  • 雪燕 - The name of the daughter, which may have symbolic meaning (e.g., like a snowy swallow).
  • 海棠 - Hawthorn or crabapple, a type of flower mentioned in the text.
  • 胆瓶 - A type of vase, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe elegant floral arrangements.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 次日 - Means 'the next day.'
  • 折简 - A polite way of inviting someone, often used in formal or literary contexts.
  • 治具召客 - Means 'to prepare a feast and invite guests.'
  • 花侣闻之皆吊湘 - '花侣' refers to a group of friends or companions, often used metaphorically. '吊湘' means 'to mourn for Xiang,' implying a sense of sympathy or lamentation.
  • 车马阗咽 - Describes a scene of crowded vehicles and people, indicating a bustling atmosphere.
  • 冠履坌集 - Means 'people in hats and shoes are densely gathered,' emphasizing the large number of guests.
  • 酒肴洊至 - Means 'wine and food are continuously arriving,' indicating a lavish feast.
  • 熏腾如毒雾 - Describes the strong and overwhelming smell of the food and drink, likening it to a toxic fog.
  • 酒酣赋诗 - Means 'drinking to intoxication and composing poetry,' a common practice in ancient Chinese feasts.
  • 评赞呶杂 - Describes a noisy and chaotic scene of praising and criticizing.
  • 湘不能堪 - Means 'Xiang could not bear it,' indicating that Xiang was overwhelmed by the situation.
  • 日暮，各选条折枝而去 - '日暮' means 'at sunset,' '各选条折枝' means 'each selecting branches to take away,' and '而去' means 'and left.' This phrase suggests a ritual or custom of taking branches as a keepsake.
  • 晚风芆起 - Means 'the evening wind began to blow,' indicating a change in the atmosphere.
  • 落片惊飞 - Describes leaves falling and flying away in surprise.
  • 封家姨来何暮也 - '封家姨' refers to a specific person, possibly a close friend or family member. '来何暮也' means 'why did you come so late?' expressing surprise or disappointment.
  • 是夕遂病，日就槁以死 - '是夕' means 'that night,' '遂病' means 'became ill,' '日就槁以死' means 'gradually withered and died.'
  • 女感其情，亦从之 - '女' means 'the woman,' '感其情' means 'was moved by his feelings,' '亦从之' means 'also followed him.'
  • 见夫人，夫人慰恤之甚厚 - '见夫人' means 'met the lady,' '夫人慰恤之甚厚' means 'the lady comforted and showed great concern for her.'
  • 复与女同生者数世，事不能详 - '复与女同生者数世' means 'they were reborn together for several generations,' '事不能详' means 'the details cannot be fully explained.'
  • 一日，夫人谓之曰 - '一日' means 'one day,' '夫人谓之曰' means 'the lady told her.'
  • 君尝欲现女人身，今当如志 - '君' means 'you,' '尝欲' means 'once desired,' '现女人身' means 'to appear as a woman,' '今当如志' means 'now it will be as you wished.'
  • 天地绮丽之气，名花美人，分而有之 - '天地绮丽之气' means 'the beautiful and luxurious atmosphere of heaven and earth,' '名花美人' means 'famous flowers and beautiful women,' '分而有之' means 'they are divided and shared.'
  • 此行无异登仙也 - '此行' means 'this journey,' '无异' means 'is no different from,' '登仙' means 'ascending to immortality.'
  • 顿首谢 - Means 'kneeling and bowing to thank.'
  • 缘深矣，可若何 - '缘深矣' means 'the bond is deep,' '可若何' means 'what can be done?'
  • 情根纠结，何时已乎 - '情根纠结' means 'the roots of emotion are intertwined,' '何时已乎' means 'when will it end?'
  • 请从此判，不复游于人间矣 - '请从此判' means 'please let us part,' '不复游于人间矣' means 'will no longer wander in the world.'
  • 遂推湘楼下，乃如自云雾中堕 - '遂推湘楼下' means 'pushed Xiang down from the building,' '乃如自云雾中堕' means 'as if falling from a cloud and mist.'
  • 形顿缩，遂为金氏女湘云 - '形顿缩' means 'the shape suddenly shrunk,' '遂为金氏女湘云' means 'became a girl named Xiangyun of the Jin family.'
  • 及笄，父母欲婿之，湘坚矢不可 - '及笄' means 'reached the age of marriage,' '父母欲婿之' means 'her parents wanted to arrange a marriage for her,' '湘坚矢不可' means 'Xiang firmly refused.'
  • 而怜钗惜粉，不异曩时 - '而怜钗惜粉' means 'she still cherished her jewelry and cosmetics,' '不异曩时' means 'just as before.'
  • 殊自忘其身之既雌也 - '殊自忘' means 'completely forgot,' '其身之既雌' means 'that she had already become a woman.'
  • 尝言作海棠时，被折甚楚，无异创其肢体 - '尝言' means 'once said,' '作海棠时' means 'when she was a peony,' '被折甚楚' means 'was cut very painfully,' '无异创其肢体' means 'as if her body was being wounded.'
  • 雪燕来折，则心悦其丽，不复觉耳 - '雪燕来折' means 'when the snow swallow came to cut,' '则心悦其丽' means 'she was delighted by its beauty,' '不复觉耳' means 'she no longer felt pain.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 湘 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure named Xiang, possibly a character from a classical text.
  • 花侣 - A metaphorical term for a group of friends or companions, often used in classical literature.
  • 封家姨 - A specific person, possibly a close friend or family member, whose identity is not explicitly stated.
  • 夫人 - Refers to a lady or noblewoman, often a significant figure in the narrative.
  • 天地绮丽之气 - A poetic description of the beautiful and luxurious atmosphere of heaven and earth.
  • 名花美人 - A metaphor for famous flowers and beautiful women, often used to describe the idealized beauty of nature and people.
  • 登仙 - The concept of ascending to immortality, a common theme in Chinese mythology and literature.
  • 金氏女湘云 - A character named Xiangyun from the Jin family, likely a reference to a specific literary work or historical figure.
  • 雪燕 - A metaphorical term, possibly referring to a specific bird or a symbol of purity and beauty.

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 年二十余卒 - Standard way to express someone’s age at death in Classical Chinese.
  • 比丘尼 - Buddhist nun; should be translated consistently.
  • 握拂对语 - Held a brush and conversed; may be metaphorical for philosophical or literary discussion.
  • 如参悟状 - As if in deep realization or enlightenment; common expression for spiritual or intellectual awakening.
  • 露珠极明，沾之立碎 - Dewdrops are extremely bright, but when touched, they immediately break; metaphor for beauty and transience.
  • 霜化至洁，触之即消 - Frost is purest when it melts, but upon contact, it vanishes; metaphor for purity and impermanence.
  • 日里霞光，非空非色；镜中花影，是幻是真？ - Poetic and philosophical question; likely from Buddhist or Daoist thought.
  • 点首者再 - Nodded twice; gesture of agreement or understanding.
  • 命瘗诸海棠之下 - Ordered to be buried beneath the海棠 (peach blossom or crabapple tree); formal or poetic context.
  • 好儇弄 - Liked to be mischievous or playful; *儇* may imply cunning or tricky nature.
  • 白纸作冠高如筩 - Made a paper hat as tall as a cylinder; *筩* is a cylindrical container.
  • 纸钱垂癴两颊间 - Paper money hung from the cheeks; *癴* likely a typo for *垂* (to hang).
  • 面傅粉墨 - Applied powder and ink to the face; suggests a makeup-like appearance, possibly for disguise or theatrical effect.
  • 衔猪舌 - Held a pig’s tongue in the mouth; unusual detail, possibly symbolic or part of a costume.
  • 表羊裘而披之身 - Wore a sheepskin coat over his body; *表* means to wear on the outside, *披* means to wear over.
  • 启门，一鬼迎面出 - Opened the door, and a ghost appeared before him; use of *鬼* (ghost) may be metaphorical or literal.
  • 与齐形绝肖 - The ghost looked exactly like Qi; *形绝肖* means exactly the same in form.
  • 合于齐身 - Merged with Qi’s body; could be metaphorical or supernatural event.
  • 大呼倒地 - Shouted and fell to the ground; dramatic description of sudden shock or fear.
  • 有鬼，死焉 - There was a ghost, he died; brief but dramatic statement, likely a misinterpretation or misstatement.
  • 亟舁之床 - Quickly carried him to the bed; *舁* is a rare verb meaning to carry on a stretcher.
  • 涤其面，饮之姜汤 - Washed his face, gave him ginger soup; traditional healing practice.
  • 病月余，卒死 - Sick for over a month, then died; *卒* here is used in the sense of 'finally' or 'in the end.'
  • 厕之鬼，齐之魂也 - The ghost in the toilet was Qi’s spirit; supernatural explanation from folk belief or legend.
  • 引镜心动时，盖离舍而先往矣 - At the moment the mirror caused his heart to stir, he had already left the house and gone ahead; metaphorical or spiritual explanation for death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 比丘尼 - Buddhist nun; indicates religious context.
  • 齐福喜 - Proper name: Qi Fuxi; should be rendered in pinyin as **Qi Fuxi**.
  • 雍正中 - During the reign of Emperor Yongzheng (1722–1735); historical reference.
  • 大兴县 - Daxing County, a real place in the Beijing region; should be translated as **Daxing County**.
  • 大兴县民 - A resident of Daxing County.
  • 白纸作冠高如筩 - Detailed description of a costume or disguise; possibly from a folk tale or legend.
  • 纸钱垂癴两颊间 - Symbolic or ritualistic detail; possibly meant to evoke fear or confusion.
  • 面傅粉墨，衔猪舌，表羊裘 - Grotesque or theatrical disguise; possibly meant to scare or mislead someone.
  • 厕之鬼 - Ghost in the toilet; common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 引镜心动时，盖离舍而先往矣 - Metaphorical explanation for Qi's death; concept found in Daoist and Buddhist beliefs about the soul.

Proper Nouns

  • 齐福喜 - Proper name; rendered in pinyin as **Qi Fuxi**.
  • 大兴县 - Daxing County; should be translated as **Daxing County**.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 狼狈 - A metaphorical term referring to a creature that is part wolf and part another animal, often used to describe a situation where two things are closely related or dependent on each other, often in a negative sense.
  • 剔目决足 - A literal action of plucking out the eyes and cutting off the feet, intended to send a message or warn other wolves.
  • 似相识 - A phrase indicating that the wolves recognized the person, adding a layer of complexity to the encounter.
  • 喻意 - A term suggesting that the wolves understood the meaning behind the actions, implying a level of intelligence or coordination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 海州 - A coastal region in China, historically known for its strategic importance and natural resources.
  • 狼 - Wolves are often depicted in Chinese literature as dangerous and cunning animals, symbolizing threats and challenges.
  • 狈 - A mythical creature often associated with wolves, believed to have a short front body and a long back body, making it dependent on wolves for movement. It is often used in folklore and literature to create a sense of the unknown or the supernatural.

Proper Nouns

  • 海州 - A specific region in China.
  • 庄民 - Villagers or residents of the village.
  • 狼 - Wolves, a common animal in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 狈 - A mythical creature often associated with wolves.

[411]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 由此观之 - Transitional phrase meaning 'From this perspective' or 'Considering this.'
  • 济狼之恶者 - Metaphorical expression where '济' means 'to assist' or 'to aid,' and '狼之恶' refers to the evil or harmful actions of wolves. Together, it suggests someone or something that assists in the evil deeds of wolves.
  • 狈策而狼攻 - Metaphorical phrase where '策' means 'to plan' or 'to strategize,' and '攻' means 'to attack.' It implies that the '狈' (a mythical creature often paired with wolves) plans while the wolves execute the attacks.
  • 《酉阳杂俎》 - Title of a classical Chinese text, which is a collection of miscellaneous notes and stories.
  • 所载事类此 - Phrase meaning 'the stories recorded are similar to this,' indicating that the text contains similar narratives.
  • 山左何生者 - Phrase where '山左' refers to the area east of the mountains, typically used to refer to Shandong province, and '何生' means 'Mr. He.'
  • 富而好义 - Phrase meaning 'wealthy and benevolent.'
  • 尝客金陵 - Phrase meaning 'once stayed in Jinling (Nanjing).'
  • 遍谒同舍郎 - Phrase meaning 'visited all the officials in the same residence.'
  • 首戴长巾 - Phrase meaning 'wearing a long headscarf.'
  • 衣甚褴褛 - Phrase meaning 'wearing very tattered clothes.'
  • 珠神玉貌如好女子 - Phrase meaning 'having a beauty like a fine lady, with a radiant spirit and delicate appearance.'
  • 见人辄扃户避去 - Phrase meaning 'always closed the door and avoided people when they came.'
  • 何心仪之 - Phrase meaning 'He admired him deeply.'
  • 投三刺 - Phrase meaning 'sent three formal invitations.'
  • 客瞰亡始来答拜 - Phrase meaning 'the guest only came to return the visit after he left.'
  • 曲突无烟 - Phrase meaning 'the fireplace was curved and had no smoke,' suggesting a cold and empty room.
  • 蝇床 - Phrase meaning 'a low, simple bed.'
  • 拥败絮 - Phrase meaning 'covered with old, torn bedding.'
  • 退问主人 - Phrase meaning 'asked the host later.'
  • 税居半载 - Phrase meaning 'rented the place for half a year.'
  • 值弗偿 - Phrase meaning 'the rent was not paid.'
  • 不能复馆之 - Phrase meaning 'could not continue to house him.'
  • 叹息不已 - Phrase meaning 'could not stop sighing.'
  • 意必避难而穷于途者 - Phrase meaning 'suspected that he was a fugitive who was destitute on the road.'
  • 代为纳值 - Phrase meaning 'paid the rent on his behalf.'
  • 嘱勿言 - Phrase meaning 'asked him not to tell anyone.'
  • 间馈之金钱周恤之 - Phrase meaning 'occasionally gave him money to help him.'
  • 床下有青布囊 - Phrase meaning 'there was a blue cloth bag under the bed.'
  • 启之，所馈钱悉在 - Phrase meaning 'when opened, all the money he had given was inside.'
  • 深叹其廉 - Phrase meaning 'deeply admired his integrity.'
  • 然心窃怪之 - Phrase meaning 'but secretly found it strange.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《酉阳杂俎》 - Classical Chinese text compiled by Sui Dynasty author Zhang Zhuo, which contains a wide range of stories, anecdotes, and miscellaneous notes.
  • 山左 - Refers to the area east of the mountains, typically used to refer to Shandong province.
  • 金陵 - Ancient name for Nanjing, the capital of Jiangsu province.
  • 同舍郎 - Refers to officials or scholars who share the same residence or institution.
  • 曲突无烟 - Metaphorical expression that can also refer to a situation where there is no activity or presence.
  • 蝇床 - Low, simple bed, often used in poor households.
  • 拥败絮 - Refers to old, torn bedding, indicating poverty.
  • 税居 - Refers to renting a place to live.
  • 值弗偿 - Refers to unpaid rent or debt.
  • 不能复馆之 - Refers to the inability to continue housing someone due to financial or other reasons.
  • 叹息不已 - Expresses deep concern or sympathy.
  • 意必避难而穷于途者 - Suspects that the person is a fugitive who is destitute on the road.
  • 代为纳值 - Pays the rent on behalf of someone else.
  • 嘱勿言 - Asks someone to keep a secret.
  • 间馈之金钱周恤之 - Gives money to help someone in need.
  • 床下有青布囊 - Refers to a blue cloth bag found under the bed.
  • 启之，所馈钱悉在 - Indicates that the money given was still there.
  • 深叹其廉 - Admires the person's integrity.
  • 然心窃怪之 - Finds the situation strange or puzzling.

Proper Nouns

  • 《酉阳杂俎》 - Title of a classical Chinese text.
  • 山左 - Refers to the area east of the mountains, typically used to refer to Shandong province.
  • 金陵 - Ancient name for Nanjing, the capital of Jiangsu province.
  • 同舍郎 - Refers to officials or scholars who share the same residence or institution.
  • 曲突无烟 - Metaphorical expression.
  • 蝇床 - Low, simple bed.
  • 拥败絮 - Refers to old, torn bedding.
  • 税居 - Refers to renting a place to live.
  • 值弗偿 - Refers to unpaid rent or debt.
  • 不能复馆之 - Refers to the inability to continue housing someone.

  • 睨视叹惋而已 - Literary expression meaning 'he looked at her with a sigh of regret and did nothing more.'
  • 既灯 - Classical expression meaning 'When the lamp was lit' or 'After nightfall.'
  • 夫人至 - Formal expression meaning 'The lady has arrived' or 'The wife has come.'
  • 门启而妪入 - Concise phrase meaning 'The door opened and the old woman entered.'
  • 邻父与生起逆之 - Classical construction meaning 'The neighbor and the guest rose to greet her.'
  • 各悲叙 - Poetic expression meaning 'They each expressed their sorrow and recounted their story.'
  • 愕然讶妪在吾家，焉得至此 - Literary expression meaning 'He was surprised and asked how the old woman had come to be here.'
  • 吾本欲俟汝为别，仆御在门，不容稽延，怏怏就道，今反晤于此，实出望外 - Complex sentence meaning 'I originally intended to wait for you to say goodbye, but my attendants were at the gate and did not allow me to delay. I went off reluctantly, and now we meet again here, which is truly unexpected.'
  • 遂述生家中事甚悉 - Classical expression meaning 'Then she recounted in great detail the affairs of the guest’s home.'

  • 将曙，邻父促生行 - Classical expression meaning 'As dawn approached, the neighbor urged the guest to depart.'
  • 各相涕泣，送生至故处而别 - Poetic expression meaning 'They all wept together, and sent him back to his original place, where they parted.'

  • 异人 - Means 'a strange or extraordinary person,' used to describe the monk.
  • 吾祖殁时，某尚未生 - Means 'my ancestor died when I was not yet born,' indicating the generational gap.

  • 访以后事，悉不答 - Means 'ask about future matters, but he did not answer,' indicating the monk's reluctance to engage further.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 比夜 - Refers to a specific night, often implying a significant or supernatural event.
  • 千山皆暗 - Literally means 'thousands of mountains are dark,' suggesting a pervasive darkness.
  • 独石室光明如昼 - Literally means 'the stone chamber alone is bright as day,' indicating an unusual brightness in contrast to the surrounding darkness.
  • 祖在，亦何畏 - A rhetorical question meaning 'since the ancestor is present, what have I to fear?'
  • 徐举指弹坐旁石壁 - Literally means 'slowly raising a finger to tap the stone wall beside him,' describing a deliberate action.
  • 壁忽辟一舍 - Literally means 'the wall suddenly opens a room,' indicating a magical or supernatural transformation.
  • 倘有所睹，勿畏，亦勿语，勿出 - A series of instructions meaning 'if you see anything, do not be afraid, do not speak, and do not leave.'
  • 今夜适有事矣 - Literally means 'there is something happening tonight,' suggesting a specific event or task.
  • 俄闻风飕飕起林间 - Literally means 'soon hearing the wind rustling in the forest,' describing a sudden sound.
  • 万木怒号 - Literally means 'thousands of trees are roaring,' suggesting a powerful and chaotic wind.
  • 有鬼魅无数坌入石室 - Literally means 'countless ghosts and demons rush into the stone chamber,' describing a supernatural invasion.
  • 僧摇首者再 - Literally means 'the monk shakes his head twice,' indicating a repeated action.
  • 小人百十，自僧两耳出，如连珠激箭 - Literally means 'hundreds of little people emerge from the monk's two ears, like linked pearls and arrows,' describing a rapid and numerous emergence.
  • 执兵刺鬼 - Literally means 'holding weapons to pierce the ghosts,' describing a combat action.
  • 鬼悉遁 - Literally means 'all the ghosts flee,' indicating a successful defense.
  • 小人复还耳中 - Literally means 'the little people return to the monk's ears,' indicating a return to their original state.
  • 复有奇鬼修修然参伍而至 - Literally means 'again, strange ghosts arrive in a mixed and orderly manner,' describing a new wave of supernatural beings.
  • 蓝身巨吻，类夜叉 - Literally means 'blue bodies with large mouths, resembling night demons,' describing the appearance of the new ghosts.
  • 僧张口嘘这 - Literally means 'the monk opens his mouth and exhales,' describing a supernatural action.
  • 则一石堕地分裂，尽化为力士击鬼 - Literally means 'then a stone falls and splits, transforming entirely into warriors who strike the ghosts,' describing a magical transformation.
  • 鬼又遁，力士亦隐 - Literally means 'the ghosts again flee, and the warriors also disappear,' indicating the end of the conflict.
  • 某战栗齿叩 - Literally means 'I tremble and my teeth chatter,' describing a physical reaction to fear.
  • 未已也，无恐 - A reassurance meaning 'it is not over yet, do not be afraid.'
  • 旋闻崩崖裂壑，砰訇不已 - Literally means 'soon hearing the collapse of cliffs and the splitting of ravines, the sound continues without stopping,' describing a powerful and ongoing natural phenomenon.
  • 有二人屹立如山 - Literally means 'two people stand upright like mountains,' describing imposing figures.
  • 僧大放两目 - Literally means 'the monk greatly opens his eyes,' indicating a supernatural action.
  • 二人忽缩小，各入一目中 - Literally means 'the two people suddenly shrink and enter one of his eyes each,' describing a magical transformation.
  • 目即闭，声亦遽止 - Literally means 'his eyes close, and the sound immediately stops,' indicating the end of the event.
  • 已而栴檀氤氲，仙乐锵然，幡幢队来，金光灼灼 - Literally means 'then sandalwood incense fills the air, celestial music resounds, banners and flags come in procession, and golden light shines brightly,' describing a divine and celebratory scene.
  • 僧即下座，作礼讫 - Literally means 'the monk immediately descends from the seat, completes the ritual,' indicating a formal conclusion.
  • 便解衲衣，胸乃洞开 - Literally means 'he then removes his robe, and his chest opens up,' describing a supernatural transformation.
  • 恍见一人端坐其中 - Literally means 'I vaguely see one person sitting in it,' describing a vision.
  • 僧遂冉冉升空，俄顷而杳，天亦曙矣 - Literally means 'the monk then slowly rises into the air, disappears in a moment, and the sky also becomes light,' indicating the end of the event and the dawn of a new day.
  • 某出，拾得履一双 - Literally means 'I leave and find a pair of shoes,' describing a physical discovery.
  • 归白其父 - Literally means 'return and tell my father,' indicating a report to a parent.
  • 父见履泣曰 - Literally means 'my father sees the shoes and cries,' describing an emotional reaction.
  • 尔祖敛时所著也 - Literally means 'it is what your ancestor wore at the time of his burial,' indicating the significance of the shoes.

  • 衲衣 - Refers to a monk's robe, often associated with spiritual or supernatural figures.
  • 敛时 - Refers to the time of burial, often associated with ancestral worship and spiritual significance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 行十余里 - Common way to indicate distance in Classical Chinese, meaning 'walked several miles.'

  • 丛树间得穴焉 - Descriptive phrase indicating that a cave was found among the trees.
  • 进之甚暗 - Describes the darkness inside the cave.
  • 半里许有光 - Indicates that there was light about half a mile further in.
  • 既而屋舍忽见 - Means 'soon after, houses were suddenly seen,' suggesting a transition from the cave to a more structured environment.
  • 客引韩入内 - Means 'the guest led Han inside,' indicating a welcoming gesture.
  • 多虬须彪眼 - Describes the physical appearance of the people present, with '虬须' meaning 'curling beard' and '彪眼' meaning 'bold eyes.'
  • 衣巾伟岸 - Means 'dressed in grand attire,' suggesting a formal or dignified appearance.
  • 见客起问 - Means 'they rose to greet the guest,' indicating a respectful reception.

  • 客滑稽数语 - Means 'the guest spoke a few slippery words,' suggesting evasive or ambiguous speech.
  • 众目韩 - Means 'the crowd looked at Han,' indicating attention or scrutiny.
  • 皆大笑 - Means 'all laughed,' suggesting a shared amusement or reaction.
  • 酒馔堆垛 - Describes the abundance of food and drink, with '堆垛' meaning 'piled up.'
  • 规地而会 - Means 'gathered on the ground,' suggesting a casual or informal meeting.
  • 序列兄弟之次 - Means 'arranged in order of seniority,' indicating a hierarchical seating arrangement.
  • 以韩殿席焉 - Means 'with Han at the end of the seating,' suggesting a position of lesser importance.
  • 饮啖甚壮 - Means 'drank and ate heartily,' indicating a lively and indulgent atmosphere.
  • 言论豪猾 - Means 'spoke boldly and recklessly,' suggesting a boisterous and unrestrained conversation.
  • 比暮 - Means 'by evening,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 华灯明炬 - Describes the lighting, with '华灯' meaning 'brilliant lamps' and '明炬' meaning 'bright torches.'
  • 照烛林野 - Means 'illuminating the forest and fields,' suggesting a widespread and bright illumination.
  • 夜中纵横醉卧 - Means 'lying drunk and sprawled at night,' indicating a state of intoxication and relaxation.
  • 手足相枕藉 - Means 'hands and feet intertwined,' suggesting a close and intimate physical arrangement.
  • 韩憖憖然莫知所为 - Means 'Han felt uneasy and did not know what to do,' indicating a sense of discomfort or confusion.
  • 竟夕不成寐 - Means 'all night without sleep,' indicating a sleepless night.
  • 明日众醒 - Means 'the next morning when everyone woke up,' indicating the passage of time and a new day.
  • 客为韩请 - Means 'the guest spoke on Han's behalf,' indicating an intervention or plea.
  • 此人尚有母，贫不能养 - Means 'this person still has a mother, poor and unable to support her,' indicating a situation of hardship.
  • 殊可矜悯 - Means 'truly deserving of pity,' indicating sympathy or compassion.
  • 宜少资润之 - Means 'should be given some financial support,' indicating a request for assistance.
  • 各探囊出金钱珠贝不等 - Means 'each reached into their pockets and took out money and pearls of varying amounts,' indicating a collective contribution.
  • 无虑数千金 - Means 'no less than several thousand coins,' indicating a substantial amount.
  • 以授韩 - Means 'and gave it to Han,' indicating the transfer of the contribution.
  • 韩惶恐拜谢 - Means 'Han was terrified and bowed in thanks,' indicating a response of fear and gratitude.
  • 视尔马弱 - Means 'seeing your horse is weak,' indicating a concern about the condition of the horse.
  • 焉能致此归 - Means 'how can it carry you home,' indicating a question about the horse's ability.
  • 吾仍送尔行 - Means 'I will still send you on your way,' indicating a continued offer of assistance.
  • 一人曰 - Means 'one person said,' indicating a specific speaker.
  • 我曹亦散耳 - Means 'we are also dispersing,' indicating a temporary gathering.
  • 后二年当期于山东 - Means 'we will meet again in two years in Shandong,' indicating a future reunion.
  • 遂拱手各去 - Means 'they bowed and left,' indicating a formal departure.
  • 或独行，或侣行，或东或西 - Means 'some went alone, some in pairs, some east, some west,' indicating diverse actions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甲歸告諸婦，婦不可 - 甲 returns and informs his wives, but the wives refuse.
  • 甲無以謝之，固強婦 - 甲 has no way to thank them, so he persistently pressures his wives.
  • 時甲兄養疾於外 - 甲’s brother is staying outside to care for an illness.
  • 嫂不節 - The sister-in-law is not virtuous.
  • 請說嫂而代之 - 甲’s wife suggests she will persuade the sister-in-law to take her place.
  • 乃詭言甲實他往 - She lies, saying 甲 has actually gone elsewhere.
  • 己苦畏，挽嫂共宿 - She claims to be afraid and invites the sister-in-law to stay with her.
  • 故為媟語以蕩之 - She uses lewd talk to seduce her.
  • 伺其已寢，托如廁暫出 - She waits until the sister-in-law is asleep and pretends to go to the toilet.
  • 使甲以乙往，而己之嫂室宿焉 - She sends 甲 to go to 乙’s place while she stays in her sister-in-law’s room.
  • 於是嫂竟與乙狎 - The sister-in-law ends up having an affair with 乙.
  • 甲亦趨乙家，尋其故歡 - 甲 also goes to 乙’s house to resume their previous relationship.
  • 是夜甲兄忽歸 - That night, 甲’s brother suddenly returns.
  • 甲婦已熟寢，其扉闔而弗遂 - 甲’s wife is already sound asleep, and the door is closed but not locked.
  • 甲兄入，徑登床，以為妻也，啟衾而憑焉 - 甲’s brother enters, goes straight to the bed, thinking it is his wife, and undresses.
  • 既亂，乃知為弟婦，各秘之 - After realizing it is his sister-in-law, they both keep it secret.
  • 其後甲乙隙於末，交相抉露，以為谤，事遂彰 - Later, 甲 and 乙 have a falling out, and they expose each other, leading to scandal.
  • 非非子曰：以媸易妍，甲誠巧矣 - 非非子 says: 'By substituting ugliness for beauty, 甲 is indeed clever.'
  • 以姒易娣，甲妻又巧矣 - 'By substituting the sister-in-law for the wife, 甲’s wife is also clever.'
  • 孰知冥冥中更有巧於易者哉 - Who knows what more clever tricks are happening in the shadows?
  • 天道耶？鬼神之戲耶？吁，可畏也 - Is it the will of heaven? Or a game of the gods? Ah, it is truly frightening!
  • 石先生 - Refers to a person named 'Shi.'

  • 既出，走入荒谷中 - After that, he went into the wilderness; a metaphorical or literal retreat from society.
  • 我其已夫 - I am finished, I suppose; a resigned statement of defeat.
  • 雖然，吾頃則陶焉而樂，且施於人矣，何憂而不死！ - Even so, I have recently been joyful and have given to others; why then should I still worry and not die? A reflection on peace and release.
  • 睨其旁有枯樹，解束帶掛焉 - He looked around and saw a dead tree nearby; he took off his sash and hung it on it; a symbolic act of surrender.
  • 咄！已而，若亦淺之乎窺世，褊之乎其自處矣 - Hmph! Be quiet, for you have only a shallow understanding of the world, and your self-view is narrow; a rebuke from the tree spirit.
  • 余不尔畏，余將與若遊 - I do not fear you; I will go with you on your journey; a defiant response to the tree spirit.
  • 若將以餘為鬼耶？否否！途適丐於爾者也，而知爾，故來 - Do you think I am a ghost? No, no! I have been a beggar on this path for many centuries, and I have come to know you, so I have come; a revelation from the tree spirit.
  • 丐於人間者，今數百年矣，而不死 - I have been a beggar among people for several centuries, and yet I have not died; a statement of the tree spirit’s long life.
  • 而爾曷以死？爾以亟死為達，余不以為能 - And you, why do you wish to die? You think that dying quickly is wise, but I do not consider it an achievement; a philosophical contrast.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 郭生 - A name of a character, likely a commoner or scholar, who is the protagonist of the story.
  • 鬻其文 - Selling one’s writings; in classical Chinese society, this could be a metaphor for seeking patronage or recognition.
  • 佣书 - Working as a scribe or copyist; a common occupation for the poor, especially in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 质衣装 - Pawning clothes; common among the poor in classical Chinese society.
  • 酒家 - A wine house or tavern; often places of entertainment, but also of excess and despair.
  • 丐者 - Beggars; often seen as unfortunate or cursed in classical Chinese society.
  • 荒谷 - A wilderness or remote valley; symbolizes isolation, despair, or final surrender.
  • 枯树 - A dead tree; in classical Chinese symbolism, represents death, decay, or the end of life.
  • 束带 - A sash or belt; often used in classical Chinese as a symbol of status or dignity.
  • 魅乎莫余诮 - Implies the speaker is addressing a spirit or supernatural being; '魅' can mean a spirit or demon.
  • 丐于人间者 - A beggar who has lived among people for centuries; suggests the supernatural or immortal nature of the tree spirit.
  • 数百年 - Several centuries; indicates the tree spirit has been alive or existing for a very long time.
  • 亟死为达 - To die quickly is wisdom; a philosophical stance on death, possibly influenced by Daoist or Buddhist views.
  • 余不以为能 - I do not consider it an achievement; the tree spirit holds a different view of death.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子仙乎哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Are you a sage?' or 'Are you a divine being?' used to question the speaker's status or wisdom.
  • 何穷而寿也 - Means 'Why are you poor yet long-lived?' This phrase highlights the paradox of poverty and longevity.
  • 且恶乎置余 - Means 'And where will you place me?' This is a question about the speaker's fate or position.
  • 回而志，作而气 - Means 'Turn your mind, and act with spirit.' This is a command to reflect and take action.
  • 将告尔 - Means 'I will tell you' or 'I will inform you.'
  • 有虎于此 - Means 'There is a tiger here' or 'Suppose there is a tiger here.'
  • 度其咥己也 - Means 'If it bites you.' This is a conditional statement about the tiger's action.
  • 因投之 - Means 'So you throw yourself into it.' This suggests a rash or impulsive action.
  • 孰与走而避之乎 - Means 'Which is better, to run and avoid it?' This is a comparative question about the better course of action.
  • 济于水 - Means 'Crossing a river.'
  • 惧不免于溺 - Means 'Afraid of not escaping drowning.' This expresses fear of a dangerous situation.
  • 因自沉 - Means 'So you drown yourself.' This suggests a rash or impulsive action.
  • 孰与方舟而纵其行乎 - Means 'Which is better, to use a boat and let it go?' This is a comparative question about the better course of action.
  • 且若今日死 - Means 'And if you die today.'
  • 其余之年非若之有也 - Means 'The rest of your years are not yours.' This suggests that life after death is not under one's control.
  • 盍听我 - Means 'Why not listen to me?' This is a rhetorical question urging the listener to pay attention.
  • 听我而死，无加于死也 - Means 'If you die after listening to me, it is no different from dying.' This suggests that following advice does not change the outcome.
  • 然视今日已后矣 - Means 'But look at the days after today.' This suggests a shift in perspective to consider future consequences.
  • 脱听我而遇 - Means 'If you listen to me and meet with good fortune.' This suggests a positive outcome if advice is followed.
  • 孰与听我 - Means 'Which is better, to listen to me?' This is a comparative question about the better course of action.
  • 诺，谨受教 - Means 'Yes, I will follow your advice.' This is a formal acceptance of the advice given.
  • 瞬间树已失 - Means 'Suddenly the tree disappeared.' This describes a sudden change in the scene.
  • 蹙额而丛眉 - Means 'Furrowed brows and clustered eyebrows.' This describes a worried or troubled expression.
  • 目无光 - Means 'Eyes without light.' This suggests a lack of vitality or spirit.
  • 鬓发蓬蓬然 - Means 'Hair disheveled and messy.' This describes unkempt hair.
  • 垢腻满颊 - Means 'Dirt and grease on the face.' This describes a dirty appearance.
  • 衣鹑结不掩骭 - Means 'Clothes patched and torn, not covering the shins.' This describes tattered and worn clothing.
  • 双履不纳踵 - Means 'Shoes that do not cover the heels.' This describes poorly fitting or worn-out shoes.
  • 生意定，走从之 - Means 'The man's mind settled, he followed him.' This describes a change in the man's state of mind and subsequent action.
  • 愚哉，若且欲死也，而畏乎 - Means 'Foolish, if you want to die, why are you afraid?' This is a rhetorical question criticizing the man's fear.
  • 随以来 - Means 'Follow me.' This is a command to follow the speaker.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 仙 - Refers to a sage or divine being, often associated with immortality and wisdom.
  • 虎 - Symbolizes danger and threat, often used in classical Chinese literature to represent challenges or obstacles.
  • 水 - Symbolizes life and change, often used to represent the flow of time and the unpredictability of life.
  • 方舟 - Refers to a boat, symbolizing safety and stability in contrast to the dangers of the river.
  • 死 - Refers to death, a central theme in classical Chinese literature, often explored in terms of fate and morality.
  • 听我 - Emphasizes the importance of listening to wise counsel, a common theme in classical Chinese texts.
  • 遇 - Refers to encountering good fortune or a positive outcome, often used in the context of following advice.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening rhetorical question introduces the speaker's identity and the paradox of poverty and longevity.
  • This command introduces the speaker's intention to provide advice.
  • This hypothetical scenario presents a choice between rash action and cautious avoidance.
  • This hypothetical scenario presents another choice between rash action and cautious avoidance.
  • This conditional statement introduces the idea of death and the value of following advice.
  • This conditional statement introduces the idea that following advice does not change the outcome but can influence future consequences.
  • This conditional statement introduces the idea of good fortune and the value of following advice.
  • This descriptive passage sets the scene and describes the man's initial reaction to the new figure.
  • This dialogue introduces the new figure's criticism and command.
  • This final action describes the man's decision to follow the new figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 及曙 - Means 'when dawn came' or 'at daybreak,' indicating the time when the event took place.
  • 有達官者過 - Means 'a high-ranking official passed by,' where '達官' refers to a high-ranking or influential official.
  • 病者也 - A direct statement about the person’s condition: 'a sick person.'
  • 遂载之后乘 - Means 'so he was placed on the official’s carriage,' a common expression in classical texts for being transported by an official.
  • 归其居而丰其室 - Means 'he returned to his home and enriched his household,' where '丰其室' means to make his home prosperous or well-provided.
  • 寻复故 - Means 'soon after, he asked about [the man’s] former life or occupation,' where '寻' means 'soon after' and '复故' means 'to inquire about the past.'
  • 尝读书矣，而未竟 - Means 'I have read books, but not finished them,' a humble and modest statement.
  • 达官悦，使传其二子 - Means 'the official was pleased, and ordered the man to tutor his two sons,' where '传其二子' means to teach or tutor his sons.
  • 达官宪令文书，一切代于生 - Means 'the official’s official decrees and documents were all handled by the man,' where '代于生' means that the man took over these duties.
  • 达官听讼久不辍 - Means 'the official listened to lawsuits for a long time without stopping,' indicating a busy judicial session.
  • 生侦于厅事之侧 - Means 'the man secretly observed from the side of the hall,' where '侦' means to spy or observe covertly.
  • 得数事 - Means 'he learned of several cases,' referring to the legal matters he overheard.
  • 某县城隍病，有为不道之诉者 - Means 'the city god of a certain county was ill, and someone brought a charge of impiety against him,' where '城隍' refers to the local deity of a city, and '不道' is a serious charge of impiety or moral transgression.
  • 胥辈利其镪，受而伸之 - Means 'the officials (胥辈) took advantage of the money (镪), accepted it, and proceeded with the case,' where '胥辈' refers to local officials or clerks, and '镪' is a term for money or wealth.
  • 罚及良善 - Means 'the punishment affected the innocent,' indicating that the innocent were wrongly punished.
  • 胥辈论罪有差 - Means 'the officials assessed the crimes with varying degrees of punishment,' where '有差' means there were differences or distinctions in the penalties.
  • 富民讼贫民之友 - Means 'a wealthy man sued the friend of a poor man,' where '讼' means to sue or bring a lawsuit.
  • 贫民尝贷富民金，莫偿也 - Means 'the poor man had once borrowed money from the wealthy man and was unable to repay it,' where '贷' is to borrow, and '莫偿' is not repaid.
  • 富民夺其宅 - Means 'the wealthy man seized his house,' where '夺' means to take or seize by force.
  • 计值为有馀，求鬻宅而益之金 - Means 'he calculated that the value was more than enough, and sought to sell the house to add to the money,' where '鬻宅' is to sell the house, and '益之金' is to increase the amount of money.
  • 富民弗与 - Means 'the wealthy man refused,' where '弗与' means did not give or refused.
  • 质于乡父老，弗能正 - Means 'they brought it before the village elders, but they could not resolve it,' where '质' is to bring before for judgment, and '弗能正' means they could not make a just decision.
  • 鸣之宰，富民阻于途，且挞之，流血被踵 - Means 'they brought it before the local magistrate, but the wealthy man blocked the way and beat him, leaving blood on his feet,' where '鸣之宰' is to bring a case to the magistrate, '阻于途' is to block the way, and '挞之' is to beat him.
  • 其友闻而怒，俟间杀富民，亡命去，卒逃于法 - Means 'his friend heard and was angry, waited for an opportunity to kill the wealthy man, fled, and ultimately escaped punishment,' where '俟间' means to wait for an opportunity, '亡命去' is to flee for his life, and '卒逃于法' means he ultimately escaped legal consequences.
  • 然贫民杀若，则怨也。 - Means 'but if the poor man kills you, it will be a grudge,' where '怨也' is a grudge or cause for resentment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 達官 - Refers to high-ranking officials in the imperial bureaucracy, often associated with power and influence.
  • 城隍 - The city god, a local deity in Chinese religious tradition, often worshipped in city temples and believed to oversee justice and protection of the city.
  • 胥辈 - Refers to low-ranking officials or clerks, often associated with corruption or abuse of power.
  • 富民 - A wealthy landowner or merchant, often in conflict with the poor in classical Chinese narratives.
  • 贫民 - A poor or destitute person, often portrayed as the victim of injustice or exploitation.
  • 乡父老 - The village elders or local leaders, often called upon to mediate disputes in rural communities.
  • 宰 - Refers to the local magistrate or county official, who presides over judicial matters.
  • 亡命去 - To flee for one’s life, a common phrase in stories of crime and punishment in classical Chinese literature.
  • 卒逃于法 - To escape the law, indicating that the killer evaded justice, which was a common theme in classical narratives about injustice and corruption.

Proper Nouns

  • 達官 - A general term for high-ranking officials, not a specific person.
  • 城隍 - A general term for the city god, not a specific deity.
  • 胥辈 - A general term for low-ranking officials or clerks, not a specific person.
  • 富民 - A general term for wealthy individuals, not a specific person.
  • 贫民 - A general term for poor individuals, not a specific person.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 竹 - Bamboo, often symbolizing resilience and longevity in Chinese culture.
  • 箨 - Bamboo sheath, a part of the bamboo plant that is shed as the bamboo grows.
  • 神仙 - Immortals, beings who have transcended the mortal realm and achieved a state of eternal life.
  • 宝箓 - The register of immortals, a mythical document that lists those who have achieved immortality.
  • 侠骨 - Chivalrous spirit, a term often associated with heroic and noble deeds.
  • 贤偶 - A virtuous partner, typically referring to a spouse who is morally upright and wise.
  • 人间 - The human world, as opposed to the divine or supernatural realms.
  • 解脱 - Liberation or release, often used in the context of spiritual or physical freedom.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '叟植笋庭中，须臾解箨成巨竹' describes a rapid transformation from planting bamboo shoots to the growth of giant bamboo, which can be read as a single, continuous action.
  • The sentence '竹忽裂，一人自竹中走出，乃前少年也' describes a sudden and dramatic event, with the bamboo splitting and a person emerging, which can be read as a sequence of actions.
  • The sentence '为君故，历此一劫，大事毕矣' can be read as a single, complex clause expressing the completion of a significant event due to the protagonist's actions.
  • The sentence '吾属皆神仙中人，以豪气未除，欲物色人间奇士，登之宝箓' can be read as a single, complex clause expressing the immortals' intentions and actions.
  • The sentence '君侠骨非常，是以在此，今姑以此宅让君，与贤偶暂住人间' can be read as a single, complex clause expressing the immortals' decision to allow the protagonist to stay temporarily on Earth.

  • 前少年 - The young man who emerged from the bamboo, likely a companion or associate of the protagonist.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '故情之所以為情，移之不可，奪之不可，離之不可，舍之猶不可' is a series of emphatic negations that must be read as a single, continuous statement.
  • The rhetorical structure '未見其人，固思其人。既見其人，仍思其人。' must be read as a single, continuous statement, emphasizing the persistence of thoughts about someone.
  • The sentence '不知斯人之外更有何人，亦並不知斯之即是新人' is a single, continuous statement that must be read as a whole.
  • The list of sensory and experiential elements '乃至身之所当、心之所触、时之所值、境之所呈' must be read as a single, continuous statement.
  • The poetic enumeration '一春一秋，一朝一暮，一山一水，一亭一池，一花一草，一虫一鸟' must be read as a single, continuous statement.
  • The series of vivid metaphors '皆有凄然欲绝，悄然难言，如病如狂，如醉如梦' must be read as a single, continuous statement.
  • The paradoxical description '欲生不得，欲死不能之境' must be read as a single, continuous statement.
  • The final statement '莫不由斯人而生，而要反不知为斯人而起也' must be read as a single, continuous statement.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雖至山崩海涸，金銷石爛 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'even if mountains crumble and seas dry up, gold melts and stones rot,' used to emphasize the enduring nature of something.
  • 曾不足減其毫末，而間其須臾 - Means 'it would not be enough to diminish its tiniest bit or even for a moment,' further emphasizing the indestructibility.
  • 必且至憾于天地，歸咎于陰陽 - Means 'it will inevitably cause regret to heaven and earth, and blame to be placed on the yin and yang,' suggesting a cosmic or universal impact.
  • 何故生彼？並何故生我？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'why was he born? And why was I born?' expressing existential doubt.
  • 形朽骨枯，神泯氣化 - Means 'the body rots and bones wither, the spirit fades and breath transforms,' describing physical and spiritual decay.
  • 情不與之俱盡 - Means 'the emotion does not perish along with it,' indicating the persistence of emotion despite physical and spiritual decay.
  • 情之所結，一成而不變，百折而不回，歷千萬劫而不滅 - Means 'the emotion that is formed, once formed it does not change, a hundred times it does not turn back, it does not perish through countless kalpas,' emphasizing the unchanging and eternal nature of emotion.
  • 無惬心之日，無釋念之期 - Means 'there is no day of contentment, no time to release the thought,' suggesting perpetual dissatisfaction and fixation.
  • 由窮而變，變而通，通而久 - Means 'from poverty to change, from change to understanding, from understanding to lasting,' describing a process of transformation and enlightenment.
  • 至有填海崩城，化火為石，一切神奇怪幻，出於尋常思慮之外者 - Means 'to the point of filling the sea, collapsing cities, turning fire into stone, all kinds of miraculous and strange phenomena, things that go beyond ordinary thoughts,' describing extraordinary and supernatural events.
  • 斯即有靈心妙舌、千筆萬墨，而皆不能寫其難言之故之萬一 - Means 'even with a divine heart and tongue, a thousand pens and ten thousand brushes, they cannot write even a tiny fraction of its indescribable cause,' emphasizing the ineffability of emotion.
  • 此所謂情也 - Means 'this is what is called emotion,' concluding the definition.
  • 夫情者，大抵有所為而實无所為者也；无所不可，而终无所可者也；无所不至，而终无所至者也 - Means 'emotion, generally speaking, has a purpose but in reality has no purpose; it can do anything but ultimately does nothing; it can go anywhere but ultimately goes nowhere,' describing the paradoxical nature of emotion.
  • 兩人之情，如是而已 - Means 'the emotion between two people is like this,' summarizing the nature of emotional relationships.
  • 不然者，男女夫妇，天下皆是也；房帷床笫之事，天下皆然也。奚必两人哉？ - Means 'otherwise, men and women, husbands and wives, are all like this in the world; the affairs of the bedchamber are all the same in the world. Why must it be two people?' questioning the uniqueness of emotional relationships.
  • 知此乃可以言情，言情至此，乃真可以悟 - Means 'knowing this is how one can speak of emotion, speaking of emotion to this extent, one can truly understand,' concluding the passage with a call to understanding.

Idiomatic Expressions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 某公 - A generic term for a certain official or gentleman, used to maintain anonymity.

  • 私一少年 - Indicates a secret meeting, which was often considered improper in traditional Chinese society.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '見其妻共男子款語' could be interpreted as the husband seeing his wife engaging in a friendly conversation with a man, which might be ambiguous without context.
  • The sentence structure '遽上常叱曰' is compact, but it clearly indicates the husband's immediate and forceful reaction.
  • The sequence of actions '某因谛视之' (becoming angry, not looking closely, shouting, and then carefully looking) needs to be clearly conveyed in English.
  • The sequence of events '后其妻私一少年，值某于寝门，奔去' (the wife meeting a young man, the husband encountering them, and the wife running away) should be clearly separated to maintain clarity.
  • The husband's reaction '某愕然，徐忆前事，以为妻弟也' (being stunned, recalling the previous incident, and mistaking the young man for his wife's brother) should be clearly linked to show the progression of his thoughts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某氏女将嫁，其母戒之曰 - Standard narrative opening in classical Chinese, meaning 'A certain woman was about to marry, and her mother warned her, saying...'
  • 婿家不可深恃也 - 'One cannot rely too much on one's husband's family,' emphasizing the need for independence.
  • 须自计以防厥后 - 'You must plan for yourself to prevent future problems,' with 厥后 meaning 'future' or 'later.'
  • 数盗钱谷藏母家 - 'She often stole money and grain and hid them at her mother's house,' with 盗 meaning 'to steal' and 藏母家 meaning 'to hide at her mother's home.'
  • 姑觉而出之 - 'The mother-in-law discovered and expelled her,' with 觉 meaning 'to discover' and 出之 meaning 'to expel her.'
  • 吾固曰不可恃也 - 'I said from the beginning that you could not rely on them,' with 固曰 emphasizing the prior warning.
  • 县中代人受杖者曰毛鬼 - 'In the county, those who took the beating for others were called 'Maogui' (Ghost of the Stick),' with 代人受杖 being a specific legal or social role.
  • 与之二金 - 'He gave him two gold coins,' with 与之 meaning 'gave to him.'
  • 楚甚 - 'The pain was severe,' with 楚 meaning 'painful' or 'sore.'
  • 急以二金赂行杖之隶 - 'He quickly used the two gold coins to bribe the official who administered the beating,' with 行杖之隶 referring to the person who carried out the punishment.
  • 杖乃轻 - 'The beating then became light,' with 乃 indicating a result.
  • 非公金为赂，杖几死 - 'Without your gold as a bribe, I would have died from the beating,' with 几死 meaning 'almost died.'
  • 虾蟆作雹 - 'Toads make hail,' a literal translation of a folk belief or myth.
  • 尝参喇嘛章嘉师 - 'Once participated in the teachings of the Lama Zhangjia,' with 参 meaning 'to attend to' or 'participate in' and 喇嘛 being 'lama.'
  • 异日见之当信耳 - 'You will believe it when you see it one day,' with 异日 meaning 'some day' or 'in the future.'
  • 止野庙中 - 'Stayed in a wild temple,' with 止 meaning 'to stay' or 'to take shelter.'
  • 视虾蟆作雹 - 'Watched the toads making hail,' with 视 meaning 'to watch' or 'to see.'
  • 张口岸上，口中皆雹也 - 'They opened their mouths on the shore, and all within their mouths were hailstones,' with 张口 meaning 'to open the mouth' and 皆 meaning 'all.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 及小酌既罢 - After the light drink had finished, a common way to describe a brief social gathering.
  • 欻然入梦 - Suddenly entered a dream, a poetic way to describe a sudden transition into a dream state.
  • 梦入于绣云之寝 - Dreamed of entering the embroidered cloud bedroom, a metaphorical description of a dream setting.
  • 心惩前事 - Heart punished by past events, a literary way to express regret or caution from past experiences.
  • 不暇他语 - No time for other words, indicating urgency or focus.
  • 欲亟遂幽欢以偿夙愿 - Wanted to quickly fulfill the secret joy to satisfy the long-held wish, a poetic expression of desire.
  • 既见绣云殊不自由 - Having seen Xiu Yun, she was not free, indicating a lack of control or agency.
  • 转辗之间 - In the process of turning, a metaphorical way to describe a change in state.
  • 竟忘前事 - Ultimately forgot the past, indicating a loss of memory or awareness.
  • 仍问“观察安在” - Still asked, 'Where is the observer?' a reference to a specific role or person.
  • 仍作潘郎 - Still acted as Pan Lang, a reference to a historical figure or archetype.
  • 仍闻鹦鹉呼“相公” - Still heard the parrot call 'Master,' a literary way to describe a recurring element in the dream.
  • 仍为绣云所推而觉 - Still was pushed by Xiu Yun and woke up, indicating a sudden awakening.
  • 仍卧西轩中 - Still lying in the west pavilion, a specific location in the dream.
  • 瞿然自惊 - Suddenly startled and shocked, a literary way to describe a sudden realization.
  • 爽然自失 - Suddenly lost and disoriented, indicating a state of confusion.
  • 复哑然自笑 - Then silently laughed to oneself, a literary way to describe a private moment of amusement.
  • 盖是夕之梦，畴昔梦中之梦也 - This night's dream was a dream within a dream from the past, a metaphorical description of a nested dream.
  • 数之前定者，卒不或爽 - What was determined by numbers never fails, a reference to fate or destiny.
  • 竟有如此梦中之梦、戏中之戏 - There was indeed such a dream within a dream, a play within a play, a literary way to describe a layered narrative.
  • 变幻于是焉极矣 - The changes reached their extreme here, indicating the complexity of the dream.
  • 金铃本名铎，金铃其小字也 - Jin Ling's original name was Dui, and Jin Ling was his nickname, a biographical note.
